title: VPN Keeps Disconnecting: Fixes for Windows 11, macOS, Android, and iOS
description: Your VPN drops every few minutes and you are not sure why. The causes are different on each platform. Here are the specific fixes for each operating system.
content:
## Why VPNs Disconnect (and Why the Fix Depends on Your Platform)

A VPN that keeps dropping is more than annoying. If you have a kill switch enabled (you should), a disconnection cuts your internet entirely until the VPN reconnects. If you do not have a kill switch, your real IP leaks every time it drops.

The root causes vary a lot by operating system. Android's aggressive battery management is the main culprit on phones. Windows 11 has driver and sleep-mode issues. macOS handles network changes differently than Linux. iOS has its own quirks around background processes.

This guide covers the platform-specific fixes, starting with the most common causes on each.

## General Fixes That Apply Everywhere

Before going platform-specific, try these first:

**Switch your VPN protocol.** If you are using OpenVPN, try WireGuard. WireGuard is faster to reconnect and handles network changes better. If WireGuard is already dropping, try IKEv2 (especially on mobile). NordVPN calls their WireGuard implementation NordLynx. ProtonVPN labels it WireGuard.

**Switch to a less congested server.** Overloaded VPN servers drop connections more often. If you are connected to a server at 85% load and it keeps dropping, connect to one at 20% load.

**Use a wired connection.** Wi-Fi introduces packet loss that can trigger VPN disconnections. If you are on a laptop, plug in an ethernet cable and test. If the disconnections stop, the issue is Wi-Fi signal quality.

**Check your firewall.** Overly aggressive firewall rules can interrupt VPN tunnels. Try temporarily disabling your third-party firewall (not Windows Defender) to see if it is the cause.

**Update the VPN app.** Old versions have known bugs. Check if there is an update before spending an hour troubleshooting.

## Windows 11 Fixes

Windows 11 has two common causes for VPN drops: power management settings and DNS conflicts.

**Fix 1: Stop Windows from disabling your network adapter**

Windows puts network adapters to sleep to save power. This kills your VPN connection.

1. Open Device Manager (search for it in Start)
2. Expand Network adapters
3. Right-click your Wi-Fi or Ethernet adapter and choose Properties
4. Go to the Power Management tab
5. Uncheck "Allow the computer to turn off this device to save power"
6. Click OK

**Fix 2: Disable DNS over HTTPS if it conflicts**

Some VPN clients conflict with Windows 11's built-in DNS over HTTPS (DoH). Go to Settings > Network & Internet > Wi-Fi > your network > DNS server assignment. If it is set to Automatic, try switching to Manual and entering `1.1.1.1` or your VPN's preferred DNS.

**Fix 3: Adjust sleep and hibernate settings**

If your VPN drops when your computer goes idle:

1. Go to Settings > System > Power & sleep
2. Set Sleep to Never (or at least 1 hour) while on AC power
3. If you need sleep, set your VPN to auto-reconnect, not just kill the connection

**Fix 4: Check for conflicting VPN software**

If you have another VPN client installed (even an old one you no longer use), it may interfere with your current VPN's TAP or WireGuard adapter. Uninstall any VPN software you are not using.

## macOS Fixes

**Fix 1: Disable network location switching**

macOS's network location feature sometimes switches configurations when you change networks, which can drop your VPN. Open System Settings > Network. If you see multiple locations, consolidate to one or use the Automatic location.

**Fix 2: Check keychain access**

Some macOS VPN clients store credentials in the Keychain. If the Keychain locks, the VPN loses access to its credentials and disconnects. Open Keychain Access > Preferences > Change Settings for Keychain "login" and increase the lock timeout, or disable "Lock after" entirely.

**Fix 3: Allow VPN in macOS Privacy settings**

macOS Ventura and later require explicit permission for network extensions. Go to System Settings > Privacy & Security > Network Extensions. Make sure your VPN app is listed and allowed. If it is not there, try reinstalling the VPN app.

**Fix 4: Disable Wi-Fi assist**

Wi-Fi Assist switches between Wi-Fi and cellular when Wi-Fi is weak, which can trigger a VPN reconnection. On a Mac connected to both Wi-Fi and a hotspot, this can be the cause.

## Android Fixes

Android is the most common source of VPN disconnection complaints, and battery optimization is almost always the cause.

**Fix 1: Disable battery optimization for your VPN app**

1. Go to Settings > Apps > [Your VPN app] > Battery
2. Select "Unrestricted" or "Don't optimize"
3. On Samsung devices, this is under Device Care > Battery > App power management > Sleeping apps (make sure your VPN is not listed there)

This single fix solves the disconnection problem for most Android users.

**Fix 2: Enable Always-on VPN**

1. Go to Settings > Network & internet > VPN
2. Tap the gear icon next to your VPN
3. Enable "Always-on VPN"
4. Optionally enable "Block connections without VPN" (this is the kill switch)

Always-on VPN tells Android to restart the VPN automatically if it drops, and to keep it active in the background.

**Fix 3: Disable data saver**

Android's Data Saver mode restricts background data for apps, which can prevent your VPN from maintaining its connection. Go to Settings > Network & internet > Data Saver and disable it, or add your VPN to the unrestricted list.

**Fix 4: Check for manufacturer-specific battery killers**

Samsung, Xiaomi, OnePlus, and Oppo devices have extra battery management layers on top of Android. Xiaomi's MIUI has a "Battery saver" setting that kills background apps aggressively. OnePlus has "Battery optimization" that does the same. Look in your manufacturer's battery or security settings and exempt the VPN app.

## iOS Fixes

iOS is generally more stable for VPN connections than Android, but it has its own issues.

**Fix 1: Disable Low Power Mode while using VPN**

Low Power Mode restricts background processing, which causes VPNs to disconnect. Go to Settings > Battery and turn off Low Power Mode when you need a stable VPN connection.

**Fix 2: Enable Background App Refresh for your VPN**

Go to Settings > General > Background App Refresh. Make sure it is enabled globally and that your VPN app is allowed. Without this, iOS suspends the VPN when you switch to another app.

**Fix 3: Use IKEv2 instead of OpenVPN on iOS**

iOS handles IKEv2 natively, which means it is more stable and reconnects faster when you switch networks (Wi-Fi to cellular, for example). IKEv2 is designed to handle network handoffs without dropping the session. Most major VPN apps let you choose the protocol in settings.

**Fix 4: Toggle airplane mode**

Sounds trivial, but toggling airplane mode on and off (which resets the network stack) and then reconnecting the VPN can fix stuck connections. This works especially well after waking the phone from sleep.

## When the VPN Keeps Dropping on All Devices

If you have the same VPN dropping on multiple devices, the issue is likely the server or your local network, not the device. Try:

- A different VPN server in the same country
- A different protocol
- Your VPN on a mobile hotspot instead of your home Wi-Fi (rules out router issues)

If the VPN only drops on your home Wi-Fi and works on a hotspot, look at your router. Some routers have an inactivity timeout that kills idle connections. Look for "VPN passthrough" or "idle timeout" settings in your router firmware.

## Recommendation

For the most stable VPN experience across all platforms, **NordVPN** with the NordLynx (WireGuard) protocol drops connections less often than OpenVPN-based setups. Its auto-reconnect and kill switch are reliable on all four platforms. For privacy-focused users who prefer an audited no-logs policy, **ProtonVPN** is the alternative, with similarly stable WireGuard support.
